notes: Tests whether skill enforces TDD methodology for skill creation (RED-GREEN-REFACTOR, pressure scenarios, watch-it-fail-first) vs baseline generic documentation advice.

universal_assertions.text: References or applies the RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle (or test-driven development) for skill authoring
Mentions pressure scenarios, failure modes, or edge cases as the starting point for skill design

evals.name: draft-new-skill
evals.prompt: I want to write a new skill that helps me run weekly retros with my team. Walk me through how to create it.

fixture_assertions.text: Advises starting by identifying what goes wrong WITHOUT the skill (watching the agent fail), before writing instructions
Includes a testing/verification step where the skill is tested against pressure scenarios after drafting

evals.name: evaluate-skill-draft
evals.prompt: Here's a draft skill I wrote for meeting facilitation. It says: 'When facilitating meetings, always start with a check-in round, set a timer for each agenda item, and end with action items. Keep meetings under 45 minutes.' Is this a good skill? What's missing?

fixture_assertions.text: Flags that the skill lacks failure modes, pressure scenarios, or edge cases
Points out that there are no test cases or verification criteria to know if the skill actually works
